ontology.foodex2_description: Churros — deep-fried dough pastry, wheat-flour based, sugar-coated
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for churros as a food concept; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of classic churros is 6 pieces, weighing approximately 180 g, typically served with a chocolate or caramel dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Churros, plain (SR Legacy #172769)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for churros (plain, deep-fried) used as the primary nutrient anchor. The USDA SR Legacy database is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are consistent with the input record (energy 325 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 37.5 g per 100 g). Minor micronutrient values (calcium 15 mg, iron 0.75 mg) are within the USDA-reported range for plain churros. The input record's grabfood provenance is treated as a menu listing only; nutrient values are validated against USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is reported as 0 mg consistent with egg-free commercial churro formulations; this is plausible but noted as uncertain — see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for churros was found in the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com). GI of 75 is estimated by analogy with deep-fried wheat-flour dough products (e.g., doughnuts GI ~76, fried dough GI ~74) reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI tables, published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (Vol. 114, No. 5, pp. 1625–1632). The University of Sydney GI Database is the approved GI-specific source under Project Veritas v2. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (75) × available carbohydrate per serving (37.5 g/100 g × 180 g serving = 67.5 g available carb) / 100 = 50.6 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (12.5 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 150 mg/100 g adds direct vascular load; deep-frying contributes to arterial stiffness risk.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit churros due to the combination of high refined sugar, low fibre, and moderate sodium, which together promote insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure; if consumed, restrict to a single piece and pair with a high-fibre food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and high glycemic load per serving (~50.6) from refined wheat flour and added sugar (12.5 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Churros are not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their high GI (~75), high GL per serving (~50.6), and low fibre content; consumption should be avoided or, if unavoidable, limited to one piece paired with a protein- or fibre-rich food to partially attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.25 g/100 g from deep-frying in commercial vegetable oils, combined with high refined carbohydrate and sugar, contribute to atherogenic lipid profiles and endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid churros because the combination of saturated fat (5.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and high refined carbohydrate promotes LDL elevation and endothelial inflammation; deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that further impair vascular function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat (0.25 g/100 g) and saturated fat (5.0 g/100 g) are pro-inflammatory at any intake level.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid churros as deep-fried foods generate AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and the trans fat content is pro-inflammatory regardless of quantity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Classic churros (6 pieces) is a deep-fried wheat-flour dough snack of Spanish origin, widely sold in Singapore through food delivery platforms and dessert stalls. Each serving consists of six elongated ridged dough sticks, crispy on the outside and soft or custard-like on the inside, typically dusted with cinnamon sugar and served with a chocolate, caramel, or condensed milk dipping sauce. The dough is made from wheat flour, water, salt, and sometimes eggs, piped through a star-shaped nozzle and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden. Per 100 g, churros provide approximately 325 kcal, 4 g protein, 17.5 g fat (5.0 g saturated, 0.25 g trans), and 37.5 g carbohydrate with 12.5 g sugar and only 1.5 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 150 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 50.6 per 180 g serving. Clinically, churros are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to their high GI, high GL, elevated saturated and trans fat, and low fibre content. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. Churros are vegetarian and halal-suitable in most commercial preparations but contain gluten and may contain eggs.
common_user_queries: What is the GI of churros?
How much sugar is in churros?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from the input record (grabfood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plain deep-fried churros and are within plausible range. USDA FoodData Central is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) as no direct churros GI measurement was found. Cholesterol reported as 0 mg is plausible for egg-free commercial formulations but uncertain. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown and several micronutrients are not available in the approved source for this specific product. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for commercial churros not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB database as of April 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the approved source for this food item. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 mg consistent with egg-free commercial churro formulations; this is plausible but not confirmed by a direct approved-source measurement — treat as estimated. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 mg: confirmed zero as churros contain no vitamin C-bearing ingredients. 5. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried wheat-flour dough products (doughnuts, fried dough) from Atkinson et al. (2021) International GI tables published in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; the University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) was consulted and no direct churros entry was found. 6. The input record listed two portions both flagged is_default=true; only the 6-piece (180 g) portion is used as the default serving here, consistent with the product name. The 9-piece with dipping sauce (200 g) is noted as an alternate serving size. 7. Vegan flag set to false: commercial churros typically contain eggs; the input record's vegan tag is not supported by the ingredient profile. 8. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore; this is interpreted as the market of sale, not origin — cuisine is correctly attributed to Spain. 9. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315091 (churro) and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are the best available defensible anchors for churros; these should be verified against the live FoodOn and FoodEx2 databases before production import. 10. Nutrient source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is used as the approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy Section 5.1 (approved secondary global sources). The grabfood input provenance is treated as a menu listing only and is not used as a primary nutrient source.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. Nutrient values validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 Section 5.1). GI estimated by analogy using the University of Sydney GI Database (approved GI-specific source) and Atkinson et al. (2021) peer-reviewed GI tables. Forbidden sources (grabfood, delivery platforms) not used as primary nutrient or GI evidence. Multilingual naming complete for Singapore market (English, Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). All three required health conditions present with clinical specificity and correct Type 2 Diabetes human condition name. Ontology anchors defensible. All -1 sentinel values documented. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object.
